ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper adapts recursive neural networks to an event‑centered tree and introduces a precision‑oriented penalty and aggregation scheme, which are interesting engineering choices but do not constitute a clearly new algorithmic paradigm. Much of the contribution reads as an application/engineering adaptation (entity masking, pretraining, handcrafted extra features) rather than a principled theoretical advance; the summary does not report ablations for the handcrafted features (a methodological weakness I penalize) nor formal justification for the precision penalty. Overall the work is a moderate, incremental novelty—informative to practitioners but unlikely to surprise experts expecting a stronger theoretical or methodological innovation. | B(3): The paper combines existing components (recursive neural networks on syntactic/event trees, entity tokenization, and simple log-ratio aggregation) into a practical pipeline rather than proposing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The precision‑oriented loss and the event-centered tree representation are useful, pragmatic adaptations but represent moderate engineering/heuristic novelty rather than a clear theoretical advance. Given the corpus-scale aggregation and visualization focus, the work is a solid, applied incremental contribution that would be expected to inform practitioners more than to surprise experts in core NLP methodology.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper adapts recursive neural networks to an event‑centered tree and introduces a precision‑oriented penalty and aggregation scheme, which are interesting engineering choices but do not constitute a clearly new algorithmic paradigm. Much of the contribution reads as an application/engineering adaptation (entity masking, pretraining, handcrafted extra features) rather than a principled theoretical advance; the summary does not report ablations for the handcrafted features (a methodological weakness I penalize) nor formal justification for the precision penalty. Overall the work is a moderate, incremental novelty—informative to practitioners but unlikely to surprise experts expecting a stronger theoretical or methodological innovation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines existing components (recursive neural networks on syntactic/event trees, entity tokenization, and simple log-ratio aggregation) into a practical pipeline rather than proposing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The precision‑oriented loss and the event-centered tree representation are useful, pragmatic adaptations but represent moderate engineering/heuristic novelty rather than a clear theoretical advance. Given the corpus-scale aggregation and visualization focus, the work is a solid, applied incremental contribution that would be expected to inform practitioners more than to surprise experts in core NLP methodology.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate classical baselines (TABARI and related rule-engine/KEDS/CAMEO approaches) and uses standard metrics (precision/recall/accuracy) on the same annotated news-title data, so comparisons are largely sensible. However, it appears to omit more recent supervised/neural baselines that would be natural competitors to an RNN-based approach, and the manuscript does not clearly report when prior methods match or exceed its performance nor provide rigorous statistical testing of reported gains. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly, but analysis of differences (when and why the RNN outperforms rules) is limited, leaving minor but important gaps in assessment. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate, domain-relevant baselines (TABARI and rule-based KEDS/CAMEO-style engines) and evaluates on an annotated in-domain test set using precision/recall, which is what matters for downstream aggregation. Comparisons appear fair (same corpus of news titles and same metrics reported), and the authors candidly discuss when rule approaches struggle and list limitations of their setup. However, the study omits contemporary supervised neural or statistical relation‑extraction baselines for sentence-level extraction and has a small held-out test set, so while comparisons are solid and the related work is thorough, a couple of important modern baselines are missing — hence not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate classical baselines (TABARI and related rule-engine/KEDS/CAMEO approaches) and uses standard metrics (precision/recall/accuracy) on the same annotated news-title data, so comparisons are largely sensible. However, it appears to omit more recent supervised/neural baselines that would be natural competitors to an RNN-based approach, and the manuscript does not clearly report when prior methods match or exceed its performance nor provide rigorous statistical testing of reported gains. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly, but analysis of differences (when and why the RNN outperforms rules) is limited, leaving minor but important gaps in assessment.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate, domain-relevant baselines (TABARI and rule-based KEDS/CAMEO-style engines) and evaluates on an annotated in-domain test set using precision/recall, which is what matters for downstream aggregation. Comparisons appear fair (same corpus of news titles and same metrics reported), and the authors candidly discuss when rule approaches struggle and list limitations of their setup. However, the study omits contemporary supervised neural or statistical relation‑extraction baselines for sentence-level extraction and has a small held-out test set, so while comparisons are solid and the related work is thorough, a couple of important modern baselines are missing — hence not fully comprehensive.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (event‑based recursive RNN, precision‑oriented loss, corpus aggregation), with a clear method->experiments->conclusion flow. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and the writing quality is described as good; figures/tables and concrete formulas (e.g. w(d)=log((1+P)/(1+N))) support understanding. Minor presentation issues remain: the treatment of the precision‑oriented α and its failed tuning is reported but could use clearer exposition, and the subjective/small evaluation of trend relevance should be framed more transparently. From a clarity/presentation standpoint the paper is solid with only modest areas for tightening. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions are stated early (event‑based RNN, precision‑oriented loss, aggregation/smoothing pipeline). Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent, and figures/tables/examples supporting the large‑scale aggregation and visualizations are effective. Writing quality is good, though a few presentation weaknesses remain (e.g., subjective evaluation of trend relevance, small evaluated sample, and some discussion of title‑specific parser errors could be clearer). From an applied perspective the clarity is strong with only minor issues to address.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (event‑based recursive RNN, precision‑oriented loss, corpus aggregation), with a clear method->experiments->conclusion flow. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and the writing quality is described as good; figures/tables and concrete formulas (e.g. w(d)=log((1+P)/(1+N))) support understanding. Minor presentation issues remain: the treatment of the precision‑oriented α and its failed tuning is reported but could use clearer exposition, and the subjective/small evaluation of trend relevance should be framed more transparently. From a clarity/presentation standpoint the paper is solid with only modest areas for tightening.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions are stated early (event‑based RNN, precision‑oriented loss, aggregation/smoothing pipeline). Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent, and figures/tables/examples supporting the large‑scale aggregation and visualizations are effective. Writing quality is good, though a few presentation weaknesses remain (e.g., subjective evaluation of trend relevance, small evaluated sample, and some discussion of title‑specific parser errors could be clearer). From an applied perspective the clarity is strong with only minor issues to address.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper uses a strong, realistic corpus and appropriate baselines and that related work and limitations are well discussed. However, both identify important gaps in rigor and reproducibility—missing ablation/sensitivity analyses for key design choices and no clear code/data release—which prevent higher confidence in the reported gains. Given these weaknesses despite solid empirical scope, a middle-but-conservative score of 3 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate — there is a realistic 3.3M-title corpus, a manually annotated set (5,172 pairs) and a held-out test (453 relations), and comparisons to rule-based baselines — but I find gaps in rigor. The paper adds handcrafted features and a precision-weighted loss yet the summary does not report ablation or sensitivity analyses for those features nor statistical significance testing of reported gains (both are required by my review standards). Related work is thorough and limitations (title-specific parsing errors, NEU class issues) are discussed, but reproducibility is weak since code/data release is not mentioned and no clear ablation/sensitivity studies are provided.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are strong overall: a large real-world corpus (3.3M titles), a realistic annotated set (5,172 pairs), comparisons to rule-based baselines (TABARI/KEDS), and concrete downstream aggregation/visualization analyses that align with my pragmatist preference. Related work is reported as thorough and limitations are explicitly discussed (title-specific parser errors, NEU class dominance, subjective trend evaluation and α tuning failures). Missing or limited elements keep me from a top score: ablation/sensitivity analysis appears partial (the α tuning issue and unclear ablation of handcrafted features/embedding pretraining), and code availability is not mentioned which weakens exact reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important applied problem and delivers a usable end‑to‑end pipeline (event‑centered recursive nets, precision‑oriented loss, large corpus and aggregation) that will be valuable to practitioners. I award a 4 because, while the contribution is primarily an engineering and applied advance rather than a theoretical breakthrough and lacks some ablations and reproducibility details (valid concerns raised by Reviewer A), the scale, pragmatic design choices, and demonstrated utility (noted by Reviewer B) make it a meaningful and likely impactful contribution to applied IE for geopolitical analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important and practical problem—extracting and aggregating geopolitical alliance/opposition signals from large news corpora—and the event‑centered RNN plus precision‑oriented loss offer a useful applied advance for noisy title data. However, the core contributions are largely an engineering adaptation (recursive nets on event trees) augmented by handcrafted features without reported ablations, and there is no mention of released code or rigorous significance testing, which reduces reproducibility and likely limits community uptake. The aggregation/smoothing pipeline is pragmatically valuable for analysts, but given the lack of theoretical justification and missing experimental controls, the work is more of a solid applied contribution than a field‑changing one.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—extracting and aggregating interstate alliance/opposition signals from large news corpora—and demonstrates a usable end‑to‑end pipeline (event‑centered recursive nets, precision‑oriented loss, large 3.3M-title corpus and temporal smoothing) that plausibly improves downstream trend stability. The pragmatic design choices (precision‑biased cost, aggregation formula, interpretable time series and maps) make the approach attractive to practitioners and applied researchers, and the corpus‑scale evaluation and visualization case studies increase likelihood of adoption. While not a theoretical breakthrough, the work meaningfully advances applied IE for geopolitical analysis and should stimulate follow‑on work on aggregation and precision‑oriented training strategies.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is within scope for a top NLP/IE venue and presents a valuable applied contribution (event‑centered RNN, precision‑oriented loss, end‑to‑end aggregation). I rate it a 4 because, while the work is highly relevant and practically useful, its novelty is incremental and the empirical reporting (ablation of manual features, code/data release, and significance testing) is incomplete, making it better suited to a venue emphasizing applied systems and empirical impact rather than purely theoretical advances.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a recognizable NLP IE problem (relation extraction/aggregation from news) and uses methods (recursive neural nets, precision-oriented loss, corpus aggregation) that are of interest to a top NLP audience, so it is broadly in scope. However, the contribution is largely an engineering adaptation (event‑centered RNN + handcrafted features) and the summary does not report ablation analyses for the manual features nor release of code/data — both of which my reviewer persona treats as important for top‑tier acceptance. Evaluation appears standard (precision/recall/accuracy) but lacks mention of statistical significance testing for claimed gains, making the experimental claims less compelling for a flagship venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This is a strong applied NLP paper that fits the scope of a top venue: it tackles relation extraction on a large news corpus (3.3M titles), proposes an event‑centered recursive neural approach, and importantly demonstrates end‑to‑end utility by aggregating and smoothing extracted relations into interpretable time series and visualizations. The precision‑oriented loss and pragmatic feature engineering align with my emphasis on deployable systems, and evaluation uses standard metrics and reasonable baselines (TABARI/rule engines). My only reservation is that modeling novelty appears incremental rather than radical, so while the work is highly relevant and useful to the NLP/IE community, it is a slightly better fit for a venue valuing applied systems and empirical impact rather than purely theoretical advances.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear claims, uses a sensible methodology, and provides experimental comparisons showing improvements over rule‑based baselines. However, common methodological weaknesses — small test set, reliance on headlines with parsing errors, lack of ablation studies and statistical testing, apparent instability of the precision penalty, and no code/data release — weaken the technical validity of the experimental conclusions. These issues do not invalidate the contribution but justify a conservative, minor‑to‑moderate concerns score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear claims and a sensible methodology (event‑centered recursive nets, precision‑weighted loss, aggregation), and compares to relevant rule baselines (TABARI), so there is experimental evidence supporting improvements. However, important methodological gaps affect technical validity: handcrafted features are introduced but no ablation analysis is reported to justify each feature (violates my reproducibility/feature‑justification criterion), no statistical significance testing of reported gains is mentioned, and code/data release is not reported. Together with noted instability of the precision penalty (failure to find α for one model) and title‑specific parsing errors, these issues leave some experimental conclusions insufficiently supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides experimental evidence comparing against relevant rule-based baselines and a reasonable precision‑oriented loss to support downstream aggregation—an applied pipeline that plausibly improves extract+aggregate utility. Mathematical elements are simple and appear correct; the experimental methodology is generally sound (pretrained embeddings, baselines, and reported hyperparameters), but the evaluation is weakened by a small labeled test set (453 relations), reliance solely on headlines (known parsing issues), and subjective/limited trend validation. These limitations are acknowledged by the authors and do not fatally undermine the main technical claims, but they justify a minor‑issues rating rather than full technical flawless.

main_claims: An event-based recursive neural network approach improves sentence-level extraction of alliance and opposition relations between countries in English news titles.
A precision-oriented cost function biases the classifier towards higher precision to improve downstream multi-document aggregation.
Aggregating sentence-level relations across a large corpus and smoothing over time produces reliable numerical trends and visualizations of geopolitical relationships.
_summary.methodology: They adapt recursive neural networks to an event-centered tree representation (replacing entities with generic tokens), add handcrafted extra features, pre-train word embeddings, and use a precision-oriented cross-entropy penalty (α>1) to penalize false positives; extracted sentence relations are aggregated per day with w(d)=log((1+P(d))/(1+N(d))) and smoothed over a temporal window for visualization.
datasets: Corpus of 3.3 million English news titles (2013–early 2016)
Manually annotated dataset: 5172 annotated entity pairs from 2166 titles
limitations: Using only titles causes higher parser error rates due to title-specific syntax.
The NEU class is much more represented and considered useless for aggregation.
Evaluating trend relevance is subjective and the evaluation used a small number of tested instances.
They failed to find a suitable α value for the FT model.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - relation extraction / information extraction (geopolitical alliance/opposition extraction)

reviewer_a.name: Theory‑First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning and computational linguistics with a focus on model interpretability and formal guarantees for learning algorithms.
traits: Subtracts 1–2 points from novelty if algorithmic contribution is primarily an engineering adaptation (e.g., applying existing RNNs to a new input encoding) without a formal or theoretical justification.
Penalizes papers that rely on handcrafted features unless each feature has an ablation and quantitative justification (minus 1 point per missing ablation for major features).
Requires statistical significance testing for reported gains; if none are provided, reduces empirical-score by 1 point.
Demands reproducibility: deducts 1 point if code and data (or clear data-processing scripts) are not released.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I prioritize principled model contributions, clear ablation/significance evidence, and reproducibility; I downgrade applied/empirical claims that lack theoretical grounding or rigorous empirical controls.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Pragmatist
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in applied NLP and information extraction with extensive experience deploying IE systems for geopolitical and social science research.
traits: Gives +1 score for papers that demonstrate a clearly useful applied pipeline (large-scale extraction + temporal aggregation + visualization) and show plausibility on real corpora.

Rewards pragmatic design choices that improve downstream utility (e.g., precision‑oriented loss) even if they're heuristic, provided the downstream effect is demonstrated (+1 if aggregation shows improved stability/usability).
Tolerates handcrafted features when accompanied by end-to-end performance gains; will only request ablations as secondary (no automatic deduction if ablations are partial).
Credits work that produces interpretable outputs (time series, maps) and provides value to domain users (+1 for meaningful visualizations and case studies).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I favor practical, well‑engineered solutions that address a clear real-world need and demonstrate usability at scale, even if the modeling novelty is modest.
